assets.name: lecture_01_figure_01.png
assets.rationale: All four candidates are title-card frames showing the Stanford University intro rather than lecture blackboard content, equations, diagrams, or board layout. They are not useful for note writing and should be rejected.

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: This is the most useful of the four frames because it preserves the clearest overall board layout while also showing the equation being written. The earlier candidates contain only the bare spacetime axes, whereas this one adds the beginning of the displayed relation on the right, making it the most advanced and subtitle-aligned documentary snapshot of the moment.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: trajectory given by x equals ct
assets.caption_hint: spacetime axes with the light trajectory equation

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_03.png
assets.subtitle_excerpt: function of time and that's the trajectory x equals vt
assets.caption_hint: function of time and that's the trajectory x equals vt

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the clearest and most complete view of the board at the relevant moment. The spacetime axes, the green light rays, and the full family of blue parallel trajectories are all visible, while the lecturer no longer blocks the origin or the right-hand worldlines. It also preserves the labels above the worldlines better than the earlier candidates, making it the most useful frame for reconstructing both the diagram and the accompanying equations.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: And his equation is x equals vt plus one.
assets.caption_hint: parallel moving worldlines on a spacetime diagram
